children.name: Pelecanus
children.description: Pelicans — unmistakable waterbirds with a massive throat pouch (gular sac) used to scoop fish from the water. The pouch can hold up to 13 litres and is drained before swallowing. Despite their heavy appearance, pelicans are graceful in flight, soaring in formation with slow, powerful wingbeats. They have the largest wingspans of any freshwater bird, reaching 3.5 metres.

children.name: Pelecanus onocrotalus
children.description: A massive white pelican of southeastern Europe, Africa, and Asia. Pure white plumage with black flight feathers and a yellow throat pouch. Breeds in large colonies in the Danube Delta and along the Black Sea coast. In flight, the contrast of black wingtips against white body is unmistakable. Winters in Africa.

children.name: Pelecanus crispus
children.description: The largest pelican, reaching 180 cm and 15 kg. Grey-white plumage with curly nape feathers and an orange-red pouch in breeding season. Breeds in southeastern Europe, Russia, and Central Asia. One of the world's largest flying birds. Classified as Near Threatened, with fewer than 15,000 individuals. Occasionally vagrant to northern Europe.

children.name: Pelecanus rufescens
children.description: A smaller grey pelican of sub-Saharan Africa and southern Arabia. Greyer than the Great White with a pinkish wash on the back. Nests in trees, unlike most pelicans which nest on the ground. The most arboreal of the pelicans.

children.name: Pelecanus philippensis
children.description: A medium-sized pelican of South and Southeast Asia. Grey-white plumage with a distinctive pinkish pouch marked with large dark spots. Breeds in colonies in trees. Classified as Near Threatened due to habitat loss.

children.name: Pelecanus conspicillatus
children.description: The only pelican in Australia and New Guinea. Striking white plumage with black wings and a very large pink pouch that turns bright yellow in breeding season. Has the longest bill of any bird at up to 50 cm. Found across Australia in both coastal and inland waters.

children.name: Pelecanus occidentalis
children.description: The smallest pelican, found on both coasts of the Americas. Grey-brown plumage, white head, and a pale yellow crown. Unlike other pelicans, it plunge-dives from heights of up to 20 metres to catch fish. A major conservation success story — listed as Endangered in 1970 due to DDT, it recovered and was delisted in 2009.

children.name: Pelecanus thagus
children.description: Found along the Pacific coast of South America from Ecuador to Chile. Larger and darker than the Brown Pelican. The population is closely tied to the nutrient-rich Humboldt Current. Classified as Near Threatened due to El Niño events.

children.name: Pelecanus erythrorhynchos
children.description: A large white pelican of inland North America. White plumage with black flight feathers and a bright orange bill. Unlike the Brown Pelican, it feeds by surface scooping rather than diving. Breeds in colonies on remote lakes in the northern US and Canada. Winter along the Gulf Coast and California.
